ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FJP covers potato products broadly; no specific code for gram-flour-battered deep-fried potato fritter.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for potato bhaji; not a clinical food entity in SNOMED CT scope.

allergens: gluten (gram flour may contain traces depending on processing)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of potato bhaji consists of 3 to 4 fritter pieces weighing approximately 100 g, commonly served as a snack or side dish with chutney.

nutrient_source.title: Potato Bhaji — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Potato Bhaji (F11090045). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database — potato-based fried snack analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for potato bhaji in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 85 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled/fried potato (GI ~80–90) and deep-fried potato products. Deep-frying in gram flour batter does not substantially lower the GI of the potato base. GL per 100 g serving = (85 × 19.7) / 100 = 16.7. This estimate is consistent with the input record value and is defensible from approved GI source analogues.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 300 mg/100 g with deep-fried high-fat preparation
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 300 mg/100 g is in the borderline range for hypertension management; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size, avoid adding salt or high-sodium chutneys, and prefer a baked or air-fried preparation to reduce both sodium load and saturated fat intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 85) and glycemic load (GL 16.7 per 100 g) from potato base and deep-frying
type2_diabetes.guidance: The high-GI potato base (GI ~85) combined with deep-frying produces a glycemic load of approximately 16.7 per 100 g serving, making this food unsuitable for blood glucose management in type 2 diabetes; individuals should avoid or strictly limit intake and consider baked potato preparations with lower GI alternatives.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (15.9 g/100 g), saturated fat (~2.1 g/100 g), trans fat (0.1 g/100 g), and sodium (300 mg/100 g) from deep-frying
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Deep-frying contributes 15.9 g total fat and trace trans fat per 100 g, alongside 300 mg sodium, creating an unfavourable cardiovascular risk profile; individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid deep-fried preparations and opt for baked or air-fried versions with minimal added salt.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates AGEs; potato is a nightshade vegetable containing solanine
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end products (AGEs) that exacerbate joint inflammation, and potato as a nightshade vegetable contains solanine which may trigger flares in sensitive individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; a baked preparation and monitoring of nightshade tolerance is strongly advised.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Potato bhaji (also known as aloo bhaji or potato bajji) is a popular deep-fried Indian snack fritter widely consumed in Singapore and across South Asia. It is prepared by coating sliced or mashed potato in a spiced gram flour (besan) batter and deep-frying until golden and crisp. The exterior is crunchy with a savoury, mildly spiced flavour from cumin, chilli, and turmeric, while the interior is soft and starchy from the cooked potato. It is commonly served as a street food snack or tea-time accompaniment with tamarind chutney, mint chutney, or sambar. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 246 kcal, 5.8 g protein, 15.9 g fat, 19.7 g carbohydrate, 3.3 g dietary fibre, and 300 mg sodium. The high glycemic index (~85) and glycemic load (~16.7) make it unsuitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes or blood glucose levels. The deep-fried preparation contributes significant fat including trace trans fat, raising cardiovascular concern. Sodium at 300 mg/100 g is borderline for hypertension. The gram flour batter contributes meaningful dietary fibre (3.3 g/100 g) and plant protein. Potato as a nightshade vegetable may be of concern for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis. This dish is vegetarian, vegan, and halal-suitable.
common_user_queries: How many calories in potato bhaji?
Is potato bhaji suitable for hypertension?
What is the GI of potato bhaji?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F11090045), which is the primary approved source for Singapore food records. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for fried potato products, clearly documented. All mandatory multilingual names for a Singaporean Indian dish are present.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for potato bhaji not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F11090045 as of 2026. potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient and moisture values are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this food item. trans_fat_g set to 0.1 based on typical deep-frying oil contribution consistent with USDA data for similar fried snacks; this is a trace estimate. saturated_fat_g of 2.1 g estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous fried gram-flour snack data as HPB does not report saturated fat separately for this entry. GI of 85 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database fried potato entries; no direct measurement for potato bhaji exists in the approved GI database. Dietary tag 'high_fibre' from input record retained: 3.3 g/100 g dietary fibre supports this classification (threshold ≥3 g/100 g per Singapore HPB guidelines).
